A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

G. W. F. Hegel

1770 – 1831

Geist, recognition, and the historical unfolding of minded life.

Hegel’s philosophy of mind is best read as a theory of Geist: not “mind” in the narrow sense of private mental episodes, and not “spirit” as a ghostly entity, but the living process by which nature becomes self-relating, embodied, conscious, self-conscious, rational, social, historical, and finally capable of knowing itself as absolute. The result is a philosophy of mind in which the body, habit, language, recognition, law, ethical life, religion, and speculative theology are not external additions to mental life. They are constitutive stages in what mind is. Hegel’s own organization makes this clear: subjective mind contains anthropology or the soul, phenomenology or consciousness, and psychology or mind proper; it is followed by objective mind and absolute mind, the latter including art, revealed religion, and philosophy.

The central thesis is that mind is freedom becoming actual. Hegel states that the essential feature of mind is liberty, that mind’s manifestation is not a mere outward expression of an already complete inner content, and that the development of mind moves through subjective, objective, and absolute forms. In the same passage he identifies the “Absolute” as mind or spirit and connects this with the Christian teaching of divine spirituality. That is why a Hegelian philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his theology without deformation: theology is not an ornamental superstructure but the point at which Hegel asks what it means for spirit to know itself absolutely.

1. Basic terminology and architecture

The most important German terms are these. Geist means both mind and spirit; neither English word is adequate alone. “Mind” captures cognition, intelligence, and will, but can make Hegel sound like a philosopher of inner mental states. “Spirit” captures culture, social life, religion, and absolute self-knowledge, but can sound misleadingly supernatural. Seele is soul, but not a Cartesian immaterial substance. Bewusstsein is consciousness, the subject-object relation. Selbstbewusstsein is self-consciousness, not merely introspection but a practical and recognitive relation to oneself through another. Vernunft is reason. Vorstellung is representation or picture-thinking, especially important for religion. Gewohnheit is habit, “second nature.” Erinnerung means recollection or inwardization, while Gedächtnis is memory, especially linguistic and mechanical memory. Hegel’s text itself divides subjective spirit into soul, consciousness, and psychology, and then treats objective and absolute spirit as further determinations of the same development.

Hegel’s account is not a “faculty psychology” in which one lists mental powers such as sensation, memory, understanding, will, and feeling as independent modules. He explicitly says that the so-called faculties of mind should be treated as steps of liberation, and that this is the only rational way to study mind. He also warns that the lower stages of mind do not remain as separate existences once higher stages arise; they become moments within more concrete forms. This point is crucial: sensation, habit, perception, thought, and will are not detachable layers but internally related determinations of one developing whole.

This also means that Hegel’s philosophy of mind is not primarily developmental psychology in the empirical sense. It is not a chronology of how an infant becomes an adult, although Hegel sometimes uses child development, pathology, sleep, education, and historical life as examples. The development is conceptual: Hegel asks what must be presupposed for a given form of mindedness to be intelligible, and what contradictions force it beyond itself. The French edition and discussion of Hegel’s 1822–1825 fragment on subjective spirit notes that Hegel never published a separate book devoted solely to subjective spirit, despite having planned a “psychology,” and that he judged psychology in his time to be in a poor scientific condition.

2. Method: mind as immanent self-development

Hegel’s method is immanent. He does not begin with an external criterion of knowledge, then test consciousness against it. In the Phenomenology of Spirit, he rejects the idea that knowledge is merely an instrument for grasping the Absolute or a medium through which truth is passively received, because both images separate knowledge from its object at the outset. Instead, consciousness itself supplies the standard by which its claims are tested. When consciousness discovers that its knowledge does not match its object, the object itself changes for consciousness; this dialectical transformation is what Hegel calls experience.

This methodological point has direct philosophy-of-mind significance. For Hegel, mind is not a container of representations waiting to be compared with an external world. Mind is the process in which subject and object are progressively reconfigured. Consciousness initially takes the object as simply other; self-consciousness discovers itself implicated in objectivity; reason and spirit discover that the world of objectivity is already a world of norms, practices, institutions, and meanings. The Phenomenology calls this the path of the soul through its forms of embodiment toward knowledge of what it is in itself.

This is why Hegel’s philosophy of mind has a distinctive relation to naturalism. It begins from nature and embodiment, but it does not reduce mind to natural mechanism. Contemporary scholarship often stresses this point: recent work on Hegel’s subjective spirit treats him as asking how natural psychophysiology can enable apperception, experience, and knowledge, while also insisting that the philosophy of subjective spirit passes into objective spirit, where social interaction and cultural institutions become indispensable.

3. The soul: anthropology, embodiment, and the “sleep” of mind

Hegel begins subjective spirit with the soul, treated under anthropology. The soul is mind still immersed in nature: “spirit in nature,” immediate and implicit. Hegel explicitly denies that the soul is a separate immaterial entity. It is not a Cartesian res cogitans lodged inside a body. Rather, it is nature’s “ideal” life, the simple substantial basis out of which the more articulated forms of mind emerge.

This makes Hegel’s account anti-dualist, but not reductively materialist. He opposes the idea of soul and matter as two independent things. The soul is not a second substance added to body, but neither is mind merely matter arranged in a complicated way. German idealism in general should not be equated with Berkeleyan immaterialism; the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy stresses that German idealists, including Hegel, were not thereby committed to denying the reality of matter in the simple sense. Hegel’s point is instead that matter, taken as self-subsistent externality, is not the truth of the living, embodied, self-relating subject.

The soul is therefore the domain of pre-reflective embodiment: sensation, feeling, temperament, affect, bodily expressiveness, and the obscure unity of body and psyche. Hegel treats sensation as the inwardization of corporeal affection and also as the bodily investment of mental states; he even gestures toward a “psychical physiology” that would study how emotions and mental modifications take bodily form, such as anger in the breast, thinking in the head, tears, laughter, sighs, and voice.

This is one of the most fertile parts of Hegel for contemporary philosophy of mind. The soul is not yet intentional consciousness, but it is also not brute physiological mechanism. It is a zone of embodied affective orientation. The body is already meaningful before reflective consciousness appears. The Italian article “Antropologia del riconoscimento in Hegel” explicitly notes that Hegel’s anthropology and subjective spirit have recently been read as advanced contributions to the mind-body problem, especially in anti-dualist terms.

Feeling and its limits

Hegel gives feeling real importance, but he refuses to make feeling the criterion of truth, morality, or religion. He remarks that feelings and hearts can be bad, evil, godless, or mean, and criticizes theological and philosophical tendencies that make heart and feeling into the standard of the good, moral, or religious. This is not a rejection of affect; it is a rejection of affective immediacy as final authority.

The theological significance of this is considerable. Hegel does not deny that religion involves feeling, devotion, and inwardness. But religion cannot be grounded merely in an ineffable subjective state. The religious feeling must be interpreted in relation to thought, representation, communal worship, ethical life, and ultimately speculative comprehension. In the Lectures on the Philosophy of Religion, Hegel says that God is not the highest emotion but the highest thought, and that the idea that thinking harms religion is a profound error.

Mental derangement and the fragility of self-relation

Hegel’s anthropology includes an important account of mental derangement. He describes insanity not as a total loss of reason, but as a contradiction within a still-subsisting reason: the subject becomes fixed in a particular phase of self-feeling that cannot be integrated into the ordered whole of consciousness. This is strikingly modern in one respect: the pathological condition is not mere irrationality from outside reason, but a failure of mediation, placement, and integration within a rational subject.

The French discussion of habit in Implications philosophiques emphasizes this point: habit “fluidifies” fixed bodily and affective determinations and can function as a victory over the contradiction of a torn self. This reading is valuable because it shows that, for Hegel, mental health is not simply control by an abstract ego; it is the achieved capacity to be at home with one’s embodied particularity rather than imprisoned by it.

4. Habit: second nature and the embodied condition of freedom

Habit is one of the decisive hinges in Hegel’s philosophy of mind. It is the process by which the soul takes possession of its bodily and affective contents so that they no longer dominate consciousness. Hegel says that habit reduces particular feelings and contents to features of the soul’s own being; the subject can move in them without being absorbed by them. Habit is therefore a liberation from immediacy, not mere mechanical repetition.

Hegel famously calls habit “second nature.” It is ambiguous: habit can deaden life, but it is also indispensable for intellectual life, moral life, religious life, and skilled agency. Hegel says that upright posture, eyesight, and thinking all require habit; he adds that habit makes religious and moral contents not merely transient emotions or abstract inwardness but part of the subject’s being.

This is one of Hegel’s strongest contributions to a comprehensive philosophy of mind. A mind is not free because it escapes embodiment; it is free because it forms a body capable of intelligent action. Habit is the bodily sedimentation of spirit. It is why agency can be fluent rather than constantly deliberative. It is also why moral and religious life must be embodied in practices rather than merely entertained as ideas. The Spanish article on habit in Studia Hegeliana similarly emphasizes habit as fundamental for the passage to consciousness, the gain of freedom, and the emergence of self-consciousness, while also noting that in objective spirit habits and customs become shared social forms embodied in institutions, laws, rights, and ethical life.

The German theological article by Assel is especially useful here. It argues that Hegel’s chapter on the soul thematizes what theology often presupposes in terms such as consciousness, self-consciousness, and conscience. It also reads habit as a “mechanism of self-feeling” in which the soul both enters into its expressions and withdraws from them, giving them the shape of something mechanical. This is exactly the point: theology speaks of conscience, freedom, sin, conversion, worship, and reconciliation, but these presuppose an embodied soul capable of acquiring and transforming second nature.

5. The actual soul: the body as sign and artwork of the soul

The culmination of anthropology is the “actual soul.” Here the body is no longer merely an organism externally connected to mind. Hegel says that the soul, having made corporeity its own, finds itself in the body as a single subject; the body becomes an externality that represents not itself but the soul, and is the soul’s sign. The body is even called the soul’s work of art, with human pathognomic and physiognomic expression.

The German text makes the point even more sharply: the soul is actual in its “Leiblichkeit,” its lived corporeity, and this corporeity is the “Zeichen,” the sign, of the soul. Hegel’s examples include upright posture, the hand, mouth, laughter, weeping, gesture, and voice; he also says that language is the more perfect expression of spirit.

This is not physiognomic determinism. Hegel explicitly rejects the attempt to elevate physiognomy and phrenology into sciences. The body expresses spirit, but not in a simple code that would allow one to read character from skull shape or facial features. The body is meaningful because it is formed by spirit, but it remains contingent and ambiguous.

The transition from soul to consciousness occurs when the actual soul, through sensation and habit, has inwardized its externality and awakens to the ego. The ego excludes its natural totality from itself as an object, a world external to it, while still being reflected in that object. Thus soul rises to consciousness.

6. Consciousness: the subject-object split

Consciousness is the stage at which mind relates to an object over against itself. Hegel defines consciousness as the reflected or correlational grade of mind: the ego is self-certainty, while what had been contained in the natural soul now stands before it as object. Consciousness is therefore not the first form of mind; it is already a mediated result of embodied soul-life.

This is a decisive anti-empiricist claim. Consciousness is not built out of neutral sense-data. Sense-certainty appears richest in material content, but Hegel says it is poorest in thought. Perception already introduces universals: the object is not merely “this,” but a thing with properties. The transition to understanding seeks the inward law of appearance. Hegel’s point is not that sensation is unreal, but that sensation becomes an object of consciousness only through categories, universality, and mediation.

The philosophy-of-mind upshot is that perception is neither a passive reception of uninterpreted givens nor an arbitrary construction by the subject. It is a dialectical unity of receptivity and conceptual form. Recent Italian work on Hegel and the conceptual/non-conceptual content debate frames this precisely: Corti’s dissertation begins from contemporary debates about whether perceptual experience has conceptual or non-conceptual content, and notes that Hegel has been read, especially through McDowell, as radicalizing Kant toward an account of perception as conceptual “all the way down.”

That said, a careful Hegelian position should not simply erase the anthropological dimension of sensation. Hegel does not begin with pure conceptuality; he begins with soul, feeling, body, and habit. The right conclusion is not that sensation is already explicit judgment, but that sensation cannot be understood as an autonomous epistemic foundation. Corti’s conclusion usefully distinguishes “descriptivist” or “emergentist” readings, which isolate lower mental structures as relatively autonomous, from “reconstructivist” readings, which treat those structures as conceptually incomplete moments whose full meaning depends on later stages.

7. Self-consciousness: desire, recognition, and freedom

Consciousness becomes self-consciousness when the object is no longer simply other, but a medium in which the subject finds itself. In the Phenomenology, Hegel says that in previous forms the truth for consciousness was something other than consciousness, but now consciousness becomes its own object. In the Encyclopaedia, he says that self-consciousness is the truth of consciousness and that all consciousness of an other object is also self-consciousness.

The first form of self-consciousness is desire. Desire negates the object and seeks satisfaction, but this satisfaction is unstable because merely consuming the object does not give the self a durable, objective confirmation of itself. Desire therefore points beyond itself to recognition: the self requires another self-consciousness before which it can be acknowledged as free.

Hegel’s recognition thesis is one of the most influential claims in modern philosophy of mind and social philosophy. In the Phenomenology, self-consciousness exists in and for itself only by existing for another self-consciousness; it is only by being acknowledged or recognized. In the Encyclopaedia, Hegel similarly says that in another ego I behold myself, yet also confront an independent other; this contradiction drives the process of recognition.

Recognition is not an optional social supplement to an already complete inner self. It is constitutive. A self is not simply a private point of awareness; it is a normatively exposed being whose freedom must be recognized by another free being. The Spanish article comparing Hegel and Edith Stein captures this well, saying that for Hegel the basic structure of spirit is self-consciousness, that recognition anticipates the concept of spirit, and that the subjective “I = I” is replaced by the “I that is We and We that is I.”

The master-slave dialectic dramatizes the failure of one-sided recognition. The master receives recognition from a dependent consciousness and therefore does not receive recognition from a genuinely free equal. The bondsman, through fear, service, and labor, comes to objectify himself in the worked object. Hegel says that the subordinate consciousness becomes an object to itself through work, and that this produces a form of free self-consciousness.

This is a theory of mind as embodied, practical, and social. Labor is not merely economic activity; it is a mode by which the self gives form to objectivity and encounters itself in that form. Recognition is not merely interpersonal psychology; it is the root of social life, law, ethical institutions, and ultimately objective spirit. Hegel says that universal self-consciousness lies at the root of all true spiritual life, including family, fatherland, state, love, friendship, courage, honor, and fame.

8. Psychology: intelligence, representation, language, memory, and thought

After anthropology and phenomenology, Hegel’s “psychology” treats mind as an independent subject: not merely immersed in nature, and not merely correlated with an object, but active within its own determinations. Hegel divides this into theoretical mind, practical mind, and free mind. Theoretical mind has to make what is initially given into its own; practical mind or will has to liberate its volition from mere subjectivity; free mind overcomes both one-sidednesses.

Theoretical mind moves through intuition, representation, imagination, sign-making, language, memory, and thinking. This is one of the most underused resources in Hegel’s philosophy of mind. Hegel does not treat language as an external tool attached to already complete thought. He places sign and language within the necessary development of intelligence. A sign is an intuition whose natural meaning is overridden and made to bear an independent mental meaning. Intelligence demonstrates its freedom by using external intuitions as signs.

Hegel also assigns language and writing a systematic place. He criticizes logic and psychology for treating signs and language as appendices, and argues that intelligence gives its own ideas existence through sign-making. Alphabetic writing, for Hegel, is especially important because it brings the word—the distinctive mode in which intellect expresses its ideas—to consciousness and reflection. Some of Hegel’s remarks on Chinese language and European civilization are historically and politically problematic, but the philosophical point is that language, writing, and the materiality of signs are not accidental to thought.

Memory is equally central. Hegel notes the German kinship between Gedächtnis and Gedanke, memory and thought, and says that memory is the external mode or existential aspect of thought. Thought does not float free of names; intelligence rediscovers the fact in the name, and thought transforms representations into universals, judgments, and inferences.

This yields a powerful Hegelian thesis: human mindedness is linguistically and symbolically embodied. There is no fully formed private thought that later happens to be clothed in words. Language is one of the ways thought becomes objective to itself. This also links subjective mind to objective spirit, because language is never merely private; it belongs to a shared world of signs, practices, and recognitive norms.

9. Practical mind: impulse, passion, happiness, and will

Practical mind begins with feeling, impulse, inclination, and passion. Hegel’s treatment is not puritanical. Passion is not inherently bad. He says that passion means the subject has thrown the whole soul—interests, intellect, talent, character, enjoyment—onto one aim, and he adds that nothing great has been accomplished without passion. The problem is not passion as such, but the subjection of the whole self to a merely particular aim.

Choice is also not yet true freedom. The will that merely chooses among given impulses remains contingent and restless. Happiness, as the imagined universal satisfaction of desires, is still abstract. True free will emerges only when the will has freedom itself as its content and aim. Hegel defines actual free will as the unity of theoretical and practical mind: the will knows its own concept and wills itself as free intelligence.

This is a normative theory of mind. Mind is not only cognition; it is will. It is not only representation; it is self-determination. To understand a subject is not merely to understand how it processes information, but how it can bind itself to reasons, recognize itself in norms, and participate in institutions that make freedom actual.

10. Objective spirit: why a philosophy of mind must include institutions

Subjective spirit does not complete Hegel’s philosophy of mind. It passes into objective spirit: law, morality, ethical life, institutions, and history. Hegel says that once mind knows itself as free and wills itself as free, it develops the Idea by giving its content existence; this is objective mind. The free will must make external objectivity into a world in which it is at home with itself.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Hegel’s mind is not an isolated individual mind. Individual capacities become determinate only in larger social and historical structures. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s discussion of Hegel’s objective spirit makes the same point: subjective spirit passes into objective spirit, which concerns social interaction and cultural institutions in which spirit is objectified; the individual right-bearing subject is not the atom from which society is built, but gains determinacy within a larger social and historical structure.

The Phenomenology says reason becomes spirit when it is aware of itself as its own world and of the world as itself. Spirit is concrete ethical actuality: the universal substance produced by the action of each and all, and the ground in which consciousness, self-consciousness, and reason are only abstract moments. This is one of Hegel’s deepest claims: mind is not fully itself until it inhabits an ethical world.

A comprehensive Hegelian philosophy of mind therefore includes rights, duties, family, civil society, state, education, labor, custom, and historical memory. These are not merely “applications” of a psychological theory. They are the objective actuality of freedom, and therefore part of what mind concretely is.

11. Absolute spirit and theology: why theology is not a distraction

Absolute spirit is the point at which spirit knows itself as absolute through art, religion, and philosophy. Hegel says subjective and objective spirit are the road by which the reality of absolute spirit rises to maturity. Religion, in this supreme sphere, must be studied both as issuing from the subject and as objectively issuing from absolute spirit in its community. Faith is not opposed to knowledge simply, but is a form of knowing; worship is the process by which the contrast is superseded into spiritual liberation and reconciliation.

This means theological material is structurally essential. Hegel’s philosophy of mind culminates in the question of how spirit knows itself absolutely. Religion is not reducible to psychological feeling, sociological cohesion, or moral uplift, although it includes feeling, community, and ethical consequences. It is the representational form in which absolute truth becomes available to finite consciousness. Philosophy then comprehends the same content in conceptual form. Hegel explicitly says that philosophy and religion have the same object—eternal truth, God—and that philosophy unfolds religion as it unfolds itself.

The relation between religion and philosophy is therefore not simple opposition. Religion presents the truth in Vorstellung, picture-thinking or representation; philosophy grasps it as Begriff, concept. Hegel says speculative philosophy is consciousness of the Idea, while religion belongs to the consciousness of the true in representational form. The content can be the same, while the form differs.

Hegel’s theology is also not a simple doctrine of a remote supernatural being. He says there cannot be two absolutely different kinds of reason or spirit, one divine and one human; human reason is the divine in man, and the Spirit of God is not a spirit “beyond the stars.” He also says God is present as spirit in all spirits and that religion is a work of divine operation in human beings.

The Incarnation is central because it expresses, in representational form, the unity of universal and particular, divine and human, absolute and finite. Hegel says worship cancels dualism and restores the subject to unity with the Absolute; he also says the Christian conception of Incarnation expresses the unity of absoluteness with particularity in the object itself. In the Encyclopaedia, revealed religion is the form in which absolute spirit manifests not merely abstract elements of itself but itself, and Hegel even formulates the provocative thesis that God’s self-knowledge is his self-consciousness in man and man’s knowledge of God proceeds to man’s self-knowledge in God.

This has direct consequences for philosophy of mind. Hegel’s account of finite mind is governed by the finite-infinite relation: finite spirit is not merely a local psychological organism, but a bearer of absolute significance. This is why Christianity matters for Hegel’s concept of freedom. He says the idea that the individual as such has infinite value came into the world through Christianity, because the individual is the object and aim of divine love and is destined to live in relation to God. This claim is embedded in Hegel’s problematic Eurocentric philosophy of history, and that historical frame should be criticized; but the philosophical point is that modern freedom, personhood, and infinite individual worth are inseparable from a theological horizon in Hegel’s system.

Hegel’s theology is thus neither an external dogma pasted onto the philosophy of mind nor a mere psychological projection. It is the speculative form of the claim that spirit is ultimately self-revealing, self-knowing, and reconciliatory. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that traditional metaphysical readings of Hegel take him to offer a rational theology of Absolute Spirit, while more recent non-traditional readings challenge that picture; the debate is real, but even non-traditional interpretations must explain why Hegel repeatedly describes philosophy as concerned with God, absolute spirit, and religion.

12. The comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Hegel

A systematic Hegelian philosophy of mind can be summarized in the following theses.

First, mind is not a private inner theater. Consciousness is only one stage of mind, and even consciousness depends on prior embodied soul-life and later social forms. The “mental” cannot be identified with inward episodes alone.

Second, mind is embodied from the beginning. Soul is not a separate immaterial thing but nature’s ideal life becoming self-related. Sensation, affect, posture, gesture, voice, and habit are not accidental; they are the anthropological basis of mind.

Third, embodiment becomes free through habit. Habit is not merely dead repetition. It is second nature, the process by which bodily and affective immediacy is made available for higher spiritual activity. Without habit, there is no fluent perception, skilled action, stable moral life, or lived religion.

Fourth, consciousness is object-relation structured by universality. Sense-certainty and perception are not brute givenness. The object becomes an object through categories, mediation, and universality; perception is already more than raw sensation.

Fifth, self-consciousness is recognitive. A self is not complete in isolation. It requires another self-consciousness before which it can be acknowledged as free. Recognition is therefore constitutive of selfhood, not a later social ornament.

Sixth, mind is practical and laboring. The self does not merely contemplate objects; it forms them. In work, the subject objectifies itself and encounters its own form in the world. This is one reason Hegel’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from labor, culture, and history.

Seventh, mind is linguistic and semiotic. Intelligence gives its representations objective existence in signs. Language and memory are not external aids to thought; they are moments in the development of thinking itself.

Eighth, mind is normative will. The culmination of subjective spirit is not theoretical cognition alone but free will: the unity of theoretical and practical mind. A subject is free not by arbitrary choice, but by willing freedom as its own rational content.

Ninth, mind is institutional. Freedom must become objective in law, ethical life, and shared practices. An isolated individual psychology is therefore incomplete as a philosophy of mind.

Tenth, mind is theologically open to absolute self-knowledge. In absolute spirit, mind interprets its own ultimate truth through art, religion, and philosophy. Religion presents this truth representationally; philosophy comprehends it conceptually.

13. Major interpretive debates

The first debate concerns whether Hegel’s philosophy of mind is primarily metaphysical-theological or post-Kantian/social-pragmatic. Traditional readings stress Absolute Spirit, rational theology, and the idea that divine self-consciousness becomes actual in finite spirit. Non-traditional readings stress Hegel as a critic of foundationalism, a theorist of normativity, recognition, and historical social practices. The better reading should not choose one side by suppressing the other: Hegel’s social theory of mind and his theology of absolute spirit are internally connected through the concept of freedom.

The second debate concerns Brandom-style pragmatist interpretations. Brandom reads Hegel as a theorist of socially instituted norms and recognition. Critics argue that this risks making all conceptual normativity a product of social institution, whereas Hegel’s logical concepts are supposed to have necessity and cannot themselves be instituted by recognition. The Hegel Bulletin abstract on Brandom formulates the issue clearly: Brandom’s slogan that all transcendental constitution is social institution overlooks the role of Hegel’s logical concept of the true infinite in the very account of recognition.

The third debate concerns conceptualism and non-conceptual content. McDowellian readings emphasize Hegel as completing Kant by removing residues of brute givenness and treating perceptual experience as thoroughly conceptual. Other readings insist that Hegel’s anthropology gives sensation and feeling a real, relatively pre-conceptual role. A balanced Hegelian view should say that sensation is real as a moment of soul, but not self-sufficient as a theory of experience or knowledge.

The fourth debate concerns habit. Kantian or moralistic readings often treat habit as servility or dead mechanism. Hegel treats it ambivalently but profoundly: habit can deaden, yet it is also the cardinal condition of concrete freedom. The French and Spanish discussions of habit are helpful because they show how habit connects anthropology, consciousness, psychic health, freedom, and objective ethical customs.

The fifth debate concerns anthropology and recognition. Some Italian work has argued that recognition dynamics can be traced already within Hegel’s anthropology, not only in the famous self-consciousness chapter. This is suggestive because Hegel’s soul is already relational, embodied, expressive, and dependent on forms of externalization, even before explicit self-conscious recognition appears.

14. Notes from non-Anglophone reception

The German theological reception represented by Assel is valuable because it refuses to treat Hegel’s soul chapter as a merely archaic prelude to “real” philosophy of mind. It argues that the soul chapter makes explicit what theological concepts such as consciousness, self-consciousness, and conscience often silently presuppose. This is especially important for any theological philosophy of mind, because doctrines of conscience, sin, conversion, worship, and reconciliation require an account of embodied affective second nature.

The French discussion in Hegel anthropologue is helpful because it warns against treating Hegel’s anthropology as a complete theory of “man.” Hegel’s subject is spirit, not the human being as an empirical object. Anthropology is the starting point, not the endpoint: it treats the human being in exteriority and must be completed by phenomenology, psychology, objective spirit, and absolute spirit. The same source also highlights the role of technique, habit, body, and mechanical memory without reducing spirit to mechanism.

The French article on habit emphasizes that the soul is immediate spirit or nature-spirit, still bound to corporeity, and that habit is the means by which the body becomes sign and artwork of the soul. It also reads habit as liberation, because it frees the subject from fixation in sensation and enables higher determinations of spirit to become concrete.

The Spanish article on habit usefully connects subjective and objective spirit: habit helps explain the passage to consciousness and the emergence of self-consciousness, but shared habits and customs also become the ethical substance of a culture, manifesting in institutions, laws, rights, and social practices. This is exactly the bridge a comprehensive philosophy of mind needs: individual embodiment becomes social second nature.

The Italian reception represented by Fabiani and Corti is especially helpful for contemporary philosophy of mind. Fabiani frames Hegel’s anthropology as relevant to soul, recognition, spirit, second nature, and madness, while Corti connects subjective spirit to current debates about perceptual experience and conceptual content. Together they show that the less commonly studied Encyclopaedia sections on subjective spirit are not marginal; they are central to Hegel’s contribution to philosophy of mind.

15. Final synthesis

Hegel’s philosophy of mind can be formulated as follows: mind is the self-liberating process by which nature becomes embodied self-relation, consciousness, self-conscious recognition, rational intelligence, free will, ethical institution, and absolute self-knowledge. Soul is the natural and embodied basis; consciousness is the subject-object split; self-consciousness is the recognitive struggle to find oneself in another; psychology is the development of intelligence and will; objective spirit is mind actualized in norms and institutions; absolute spirit is mind knowing its own ultimate truth in art, religion, and philosophy.

The theological dimension is indispensable because Hegel’s final question is not merely “How do organisms think?” or “How does consciousness represent objects?” but “How does spirit come to know itself as free, infinite, and reconciled?” Christianity matters for him because it represents the unity of divine and human, universal and particular, infinite and finite, and because it gives the individual infinite worth in relation to divine love. Philosophy does not discard this content; it translates it from representation into concept.

A Hegelian philosophy of mind is therefore neither Cartesian dualism, nor reductive materialism, nor functionalist information-processing theory, nor social constructivism alone. It is an embodied, developmental, recognitive, normative, institutional, historical, and theological account of spirit as freedom. Its most important contemporary lesson may be that mind is not located simply “inside” the subject. It is in the body, in habit, in language, in work, in mutual recognition, in law, in ethical life, in worship, and in thought’s attempt to comprehend the whole.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
